name: Pulau Tekong
body: Every cohort of conscripts inherits the island's stories before they ever set foot on it, handed down in the days before enlistment by brothers and cousins who swear each word is true, until the place is half myth before the ferry even casts off. Pulau Tekong sits low and green off the northeastern coast, close enough to see and far enough to feel cut loose from the mainland, a training island of jungle tracks, live ranges, and barrack blocks where tired young men spend their first uneasy weeks away from home. The most enduring story is the tale of a company of recruits, Charlie Company in nearly every telling, that vanished on a night exercise and was quietly struck from the records, so that the bunk you sleep in may be one no roll call admits exists. The detail that the men were erased, not merely lost, is what gives the story its grip: it is not a tragedy so much as a cover-up, and you are sleeping inside it. The island lends the tale its weight; under a moonless sky the jungle presses close against the wire, the sea is only a sound, and a young man lying awake can believe almost anything about the dark beyond his block.

Then come the smaller, surer tales, the ones told in low voices after lights-out: the extra pair of boots marching in the dark, the section that counts one too many heads, the figure standing at the foot of the bed at three in the morning, the soldier who hears his own name called from the treeline in a voice he knows is not human. There is the famous bunk with a third door cut into it, sought by every fresh intake, said to have been opened on a Taoist priest's advice so a tormenting spirit could leave, though the original quarters were replaced by newer facilities in the early 2000s and the door is now more rumor than architecture. Under the lore sits a documented grief the stories grew around: on 23 May 1983 a nineteen-year-old recruit went missing after a long route march and was found the next day some kilometres from camp, his death recorded with an open verdict and no supernatural element in the record at all. The shouting voice in the night, the entrails by the tree, the spirit calling Charlie Company to fall in, all of that is folklore that gathered over a real young man's death like moss over a stone. They are domestic horrors, scaled to barracks life, and they spread because every recruit already lies awake exhausted in an unfamiliar bunk, primed to mistake a shadow for a watcher and a draft for a breath.

What lingers is how the island folds these stories into the ordinary machinery of training, so that fear becomes part of the routine: the route march that passes the overgrown plot everyone avoids, the sergeant who will not explain why a certain block stays empty, the way the older recruits go quiet near a particular bend in the perimeter track. The legend never survives in writing; it lives mouth to mouth, mutating with each intake, which is precisely why the same motifs surface independently from men who never met, the vanished company and the extra head and the named voice from the trees recurring like a tune no one taught them. Long after the men leave, the island keeps them by keeping the stories, and a new intake arrives each cycle to be told, on their first uneasy night, exactly how many of them there are supposed to be.
note: This is a rare living tradition, passed mouth to mouth through national service and mutating with each intake rather than surviving in any written source. It is worth collecting first-hand precisely because the same core motifs (the vanished company, the extra head at roll call, the named voice from the trees) surface independently from men who never met, which is its own kind of unsettling.

Treat the "erased company" as folklore, not documented event: there is no credible record of a Charlie Company being deleted from the rolls, and responsible collecting means resisting the urge to manufacture dates, unit numbers, or casualty figures to make it sound official. The honest value here is ethnographic, charting how the legend changes between cohorts, which details harden and which drop away, and why an island of tired young men reliably grows the same ghosts. Be respectful of the real institution and the real people who serve there; the interest is in the storytelling, not in passing rumor off as history.

recountings.text: The most retold barrack legend says a Charlie Company recruit vanished during a route march and was later found dead by a tree, his entrails laid out beside him; afterward, recruits supposedly heard his voice in the night shouting for Charlie Company to fall in. A journalist's investigation pinned down the real incident behind the tale: on 23 May 1983 a 19-year-old recruit went missing after a 16km march and was found the next day around 5km from camp, with a coroner attributing death to a ruptured stomach and recording an open verdict. The documented record contains no supernatural element; the shouting-spirit details are folklore that grew around a genuine tragedy.
recountings.attribution: S M Ong, Charlie, we may have company (citing Karl Ho's investigation)

recountings.text: A companion legend holds that the dead recruit's spirit tormented his old bunkmates with rattling cupboards and scratching on the walls, until a Taoist priest performed rites and advised cutting a third door into the bunk so the spirit could leave. Recruits still hunt for the famous three-door bunk during enlistment, though the lore notes the original bunks were replaced by newer facilities in the early 2000s. Separately, field-camp lore describes a recruit whose shovel struck a buried object, after which he sensed a long-haired woman with her face covered drawing slowly closer; these are collected recruit stories, not verified events.

A Singapore paranormal group documented first-hand recollections, including a former recruit, Charles Goh, who said that on his first night in 1988 he heard the sound of marching boots and later saw a sealed-off bunk with coloured talisman papers strewn across the floor, fully boarded up and very dark. The group also gathered 1983-era accounts of the search for the recruit, but concluded that the death and the third-door bunk were likely separate matters later merged into one legend. This is investigative lore drawn from interviews, distinct from any official record.
recountings.attribution: Asia Paranormal Investigators, mystery of the Pulau Tekong third-door bunk

references.title: Mystery of Pulau Tekong 3rd Door Bunk
